item.title: Acer Swift X (2024) vs Acer Swift Go (2024)
item.itunes:title: Acer Swift X (2024) vs Acer Swift Go (2024)
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
• Acer Swift X (2024)
    The high-performance model

    Premium creator laptop

    The Acer Swift X is a premium creator laptop with excellent specs. It comes with up to an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, 32GB RAM, and up to 1TB of storage. This laptop also has multiple AI features and a beautiful OLED display that's just perfect for creatives.

    Pros
    • Excellent OLED display
    • Dedicated GPU
    • Has multiple AI features
    Cons
    • No touch-screen
    • Bulky and heavier than the Swift Go
    $1200 at Acer
  • Acer Swift Go 14 (2024)
    Mid-range option

    Budget productivity machine

    Looking for a budget productivity laptop with decent specs? Look no further than the Acer Swift Go. It has Intel's latest processors, up to 32GB RAM, an OLED display with a 90Hz refresh rate, and up to 1TB storage. This laptop is an excellent choice for on-the-go productivity and is portable, well-performing, and endowed with AI features.

    Pros
    • Portable and lightweight
    • Latest Intel processors
    • Great webcam
    Cons
    • Inconsistent battery life
    • Loud fan noise
    $1000 at Amazon$1000 at Acer

Choosing between two different laptops can be a chore, especially if they are from the same brand and offer the latest specs. This is the case with Acer's prominent lineups: the Acer Swift X (2024) and the Acer Swift Go (2024). Both laptops have the latest Intel CPUs, up to 32GB RAM, a good selection of ports, and up to 1TB storage.

However, while the Swift X model represents one of Acer's best laptop lines, the Swift Go has always been its mid-range option. For this reason, both laptops share many differences that make one the winner, even though they have the latest Intel processors. Without much ado, let's dive into what sets these laptops apart to help you decide the ideal one for your needs.

Price, availability, and specs

A price difference that's worth paying for

Both laptops are currently available on Acer's online store and other retailers and stores. The Acer Swift X starts at $1400, while the Swift Go starts at $800. Given the price difference between both devices, it's only fitting that you expect more from the Acer Swift X.

The laptop is available in two models, one sporting an Intel Core Ultra 5 125H and the other having an Intel Core Ultra 7 155H. You can spec up the GPU from an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3050 to an RTX 4070 and choose between a 2.5K IPS display and a 2.8K OLED display, both with a 120Hz refresh rate.

The Swift Go is also available in two models: one with an Intel Core Ultra 5 125H processor and the other with an Intel Core Ultra 7 155H processor. However, unlike the Swift X, there's no dedicated GPU on the Swift Go models, meaning you'll be working with the new Intel Arc integrated graphics. The Swift Go is also available in two different display configurations, including a 2.8K 90Hz OLED display and a 1920x 1200 60Hz IPS display. That said, you can configure both laptops with up to 32GB of RAM and 1TB of storage, so you'll get enough speed and storage for your workload.


  		• Acer Swift X (2024)Acer Swift Go 14 (2024)
    Operating SystemWindows 11 HomeWindows 11
    CPUUp to Intel Core Ultra 7 155HIntel Core Ultra 5 125H & Ultra 7 155H
    GPUUp to Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070Intel Arc graphics (integrated)
    StorageUp to 1TB PCIe 4.0 SSD Up to 1TB PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD
    Display (Size, Resolution)Up to 14.5-inch OLED, 16:10 aspect ratio, WQXGA+ (2880x1800), 120Hz refresh rate, 100% DCI-P3, 500 nits, DisplayHDR 500 True Black, Eye Safe Certified14-inch, 16:10 aspect ratio, upto 2.8K OLED 90Hz refresh rate
    CameraFull HD 1080p webcam Quad HD 1440p webcam with Acer PurifiedView
    ColorsSteel GraySilver
    Ports1 HDMI, 1 USB0A 3.2 Gen 1, 1 USB-A 3.2 Gen 2, 1 USB-C2x Thunderbolt 4 (USB-C), 2x USB 3.2 Gen 1 Type-A, HDMI 2.1, 3.5mm combo headphone jack, microSD card reader
    Dimensions12.71x8.98x0.7 inches (322.8x228.1x17.8mm) 12.3x8.6x0.59 inches (312.42x218.44x14.96mm)
    Weight3.42 pounds (1.55kg) Starting at 2.91 pounds (1.32kg)
Related
Acer Swift X (2024): Price, improvements, and everything to know about this creator's laptop
The Acer Swift X (2024) is being supercharged with Intel Core Ultra H-series processors and upgraded OLED display technology.
Related
Acer Swift Go 14 review: Core Ultra is a game-changer for Intel
It's one of the first laptops with Intel Meteor Lake

Design

Minimalist exterior and new co-pilot keys

Both laptops offer a sleek, minimalist design, though the Swift X feels more premium, while the Swift Go's build quality feels cheap. They both sport an angular aluminum chassis, with the Swift X having a dark gray color and sharper edges and the Swift Go being silver with more subtle edges. Thanks to the larger battery, slightly larger display, and dedicated graphics unit, the Swift X is a tad bulkier than the Swift Go, meaning it has more heft at 3.41 pounds compared to the 2.76 pounds of its Go counterpart. The keyboards on both devices offer top-notch tactility, key travel, and comfortable typing experiences, so you won't have an issue on that front.

Acer has also incorporated the Windows Co-Pilot key into the keyboard for quick access to AI features, and its Acer Sense key to help you manage your computer's performance, thermals, and more. Both laptops feature the same number of ports, including two Thunderbolt 4 ports, two USB-A ports, one microSD card slot, HDMI 2.1, a Kensington lock, and a headphone jack. They also have larger touchpads that feel and work great, even though they're made of plastic. Overall, both laptops share a similar design, so if a more portable and lightweight productivity machine is what you want, the Swift Go might be the ideal option for you.

Display

Better color accuracy on the Swift X

The display department is where both laptops start showing significant differences. Starting with the Acer Swift X, you can choose between a 14.5-inch 2560x1600 IPS screen or a 14.5-inch 2880x1800 OLED display. Both configurations have a 120Hz refresh rate and a 16:10 aspect ratio, ensuring you get a nice field of view without missing out on responsiveness when you decide to catch up on your favorite games or do some productivity tasks.

Creatives are also in for a ride as the color accuracy here is excellent, with true blacks, top-notch contrast, a vibrant image quality, and adaptive brightness. The OLED configuration offers 100% DCI-P3 coverage and is Calman-verified, meaning that you'll enjoy high-level accuracy in your photos, videos, and animations. You should opt for this configuration if you're a creative looking to enjoy an immersive viewing experience and industry-level color reproduction.

Render credit: Acer

Moving on to the Acer Swift Go, you'll notice that the display, while great for a mid-range laptop, is not as good as what you get on the Swift X. You can choose between two configurations here, too, including a 14-inch 1920x1200 60Hz IPS display and a better 14-inch 2800x1880 OLED display with a 90Hz refresh rate. Both configurations have a 16:10 aspect ratio, and as you may have guessed, the OLED configuration has better color accuracy than its IPS counterpart with 100% DCI-P3 and HDR 500 True Black support. While we could not review the OLED version, we found the Swift Go's IPS display great for standard use but quite shabby for creative purposes.

From our test, we found the IPS display supports 99% sRGB, 75% P3, and 75% Adobe RGB. As you can see, while the display is ideal for standard productivity tasks like using Word and surfing the web, it isn't the best for Photoshop, Lightroom, and other creative uses. Ultimately, if you work in film and media and are deciding between the Swift X and Swift Go for the ideal laptop, the Swift X with OLED configuration should be the way to go. Otherwise, you can opt for any of the other configurations.

Performance

Dedicated GPU vs integrated GPU

The Acer Swift X's performance is excellent, thanks to the new Intel Core Ultra processors and dedicated NVIDIA GPUs. You can configure the laptop with up to an Intel Core Ultra 7 processor and a GeForce RTX 4070 GPU. While you won't get performance jumps like on the MacBook Pro M3, you'll have no problems with the performance on the Swift X, whether for creative work, some light gaming, code compiling, or everyday productivity tasks. Also, thanks to the dedicated GPUs and NVIDIA's Studio drivers, creating and gaming will improve as you can enjoy ray-tracing, DLSS 3.5, and ray reconstruction.

On the other hand, while the Swift Go's performance isn't something to scoff at, it won't operate on the same level as the Acer Swift X. While they both feature the same CPUs, the Swift Go doesn't have a dedicated GPU but relies on integrated Intel Arc graphics for its graphical processes. The Swift Go is basically a work laptop that can handle a bit of gaming, as the Intel Arc GPU can do just that. It'll play games like Forza Horizon 5 and Cuphead on medium settings and provide reasonable frame rates, but it won't do more.

Both laptops can be configured with up to 32GB RAM and 1TB storage, so you'll have enough speed and storage for your tasks and files. It's 2024; we can't end the performance discussion without mentioning AI. Thanks to the Core Ultra processors having a dedicated NPU, both machines can execute on-device AI tasks, such as blurring your background during a video call and having your camera follow your gaze. Acer also has a dedicated AI Zone that offers enhanced image and video editing features and Acer Purified Voice 2.0 to help improve your audio quality during video calls.

Battery life and cameras

Average battery life on both devices

Thanks to the Intel Core Ultra's tile design and efficiency cores, the Acer Swift X's 76Wh battery is expected to last up to eight hours under normal working conditions. This is notable, considering the laptop houses a dedicated GPU, high-quality displays with high refresh rates, and a slightly larger screen size. On the other hand, the Swift Go sports a 65Wh battery, and our tests show its battery life to be inconsistent. The best we got was a little over seven and a half hours, and at its worst, it needed to be charged after three hours and 23 minutes.

Ultimately, despite the differences in battery sizes, you're likely to experience similar battery life on both devices. Moving on to cameras, the Swift X has a Full HD 1080p webcam while the Swift Go sports a Quad HD 1440p webcam. Both are great for video calls but are not IR cameras, meaning Windows Hello won't work on either device. Instead, you get a fingerprint scanner for security and login purposes.

Which should you choose?

The Acer Swift X is the clear winner

The Acer Swift X is the ideal laptop to choose between these two devices. It's well-equipped with proper gaming, creating, coding, and productivity components. While it's a bit bulky and heavier than the Swift Go, the Swift X is still a portable laptop that can be used for on-the-go productivity. It's an ideal option if you want a premium laptop with the latest specs Intel and NVIDIA have to offer.

Acer Swift X (2024)
Editor's choice

The Acer Swift X is a premium creator laptop with excellent specs. It comes with up to an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, 32GB of RAM, and up to 1TB storage. This laptop also has multiple AI features and a beautiful OLED display that's just perfect for creatives.

$1200 at Acer

Now, that doesn't mean the Swift Go is a bad choice. It also has Intel's latest processors, AI capabilities, a decent display, and a good battery life. The Swift Go is an excellent choice if you're on a budget, not looking to play games, and looking for a simple productivity laptop.

Acer Swift Go 14 (2024)
Budget option

The Acer Swift Go has Intel's latest processors, up to 32GB of RAM, an OLED display with a 90Hz refresh rate, and up to 1TB storage. This laptop is an excellent choice for on-the-go productivity and is portable, well-performing, and endowed with AI features.

$1000 at Amazon$1000 at Acer

Both configurations have a 120Hz refresh rate and a 16:10 aspect ratio, ensuring you get a nice field of view without missing out on responsiveness when you decide to catch up on your favorite games or do some productivity tasks.

Creatives are also in for a ride as the color accuracy here is excellent, with true blacks, top-notch contrast, a vibrant image quality, and adaptive brightness. The OLED configuration offers 100% DCI-P3 coverage and is Calman-verified, meaning that you'll enjoy high-level accuracy in your photos, videos, and animations. You should opt for this configuration if you're a creative looking to enjoy an immersive viewing experience and industry-level color reproduction.

Render credit: Acer

Moving on to the Acer Swift Go, you'll notice that the display, while great for a mid-range laptop, is not as good as what you get on the Swift X. You can choose between two configurations here, too, including a 14-inch 1920x1200 60Hz IPS display and a better 14-inch 2800x1880 OLED display with a 90Hz refresh rate. Both configurations have a 16:10 aspect ratio, and as you may have guessed, the OLED configuration has better color accuracy than its IPS counterpart with 100% DCI-P3 and HDR 500 True Black support. While we could not review the OLED version, we found the Swift Go's IPS display great for standard use but quite shabby for creative purposes.

From our test, we found the IPS display supports 99% sRGB, 75% P3, and 75% Adobe RGB. As you can see, while the display is ideal for standard productivity tasks like using Word and surfing the web, it isn't the best for Photoshop, Lightroom, and other creative uses. Ultimately, if you work in film and media and are deciding between the Swift X and Swift Go for the ideal laptop, the Swift X with OLED configuration should be the way to go. Otherwise, you can opt for any of the other configurations.

Performance

Dedicated GPU vs integrated GPU

The Acer Swift X's performance is excellent, thanks to the new Intel Core Ultra processors and dedicated NVIDIA GPUs. You can configure the laptop with up to an Intel Core Ultra 7 processor and a GeForce RTX 4070 GPU. While you won't get performance jumps like on the MacBook Pro M3, you'll have no problems with the performance on the Swift X, whether for creative work, some light gaming, code compiling, or everyday productivity tasks. Also, thanks to the dedicated GPUs and NVIDIA's Studio drivers, creating and gaming will improve as you can enjoy ray-tracing, DLSS 3.5, and ray reconstruction.

On the other hand, while the Swift Go's performance isn't something to scoff at, it won't operate on the same level as the Acer Swift X. While they both feature the same CPUs, the Swift Go doesn't have a dedicated GPU but relies on integrated Intel Arc graphics for its graphical processes. The Swift Go is basically a work laptop that can handle a bit of gaming, as the Intel Arc GPU can do just that. It'll play games like Forza Horizon 5 and Cuphead on medium settings and provide reasonable frame rates, but it won't do more.

Both laptops can be configured with up to 32GB RAM and 1TB storage, so you'll have enough speed and storage for your tasks and files. It's 2024; we can't end the performance discussion without mentioning AI. Thanks to the Core Ultra processors having a dedicated NPU, both machines can execute on-device AI tasks, such as blurring your background during a video call and having your camera follow your gaze. Acer also has a dedicated AI Zone that offers enhanced image and video editing features and Acer Purified Voice 2.0 to help improve your audio quality during video calls.

Battery life and cameras

Average battery life on both devices

Thanks to the Intel Core Ultra's tile design and efficiency cores, the Acer Swift X's 76Wh battery is expected to last up to eight hours under normal working conditions. This is notable, considering the laptop houses a dedicated GPU, high-quality displays with high refresh rates, and a slightly larger screen size. On the other hand, the Swift Go sports a 65Wh battery, and our tests show its battery life to be inconsistent. The best we got was a little over seven and a half hours, and at its worst, it needed to be charged after three hours and 23 minutes.

Ultimately, despite the differences in battery sizes, you're likely to experience similar battery life on both devices. Moving on to cameras, the Swift X has a Full HD 1080p webcam while the Swift Go sports a Quad HD 1440p webcam. Both are great for video calls but are not IR cameras, meaning Windows Hello won't work on either device. Instead, you get a fingerprint scanner for security and login purposes.

Which should you choose?

The Acer Swift X is the clear winner

The Acer Swift X is the ideal laptop to choose between these two devices. It's well-equipped with proper gaming, creating, coding, and productivity components. While it's a bit bulky and heavier than the Swift Go, the Swift X is still a portable laptop that can be used for on-the-go productivity. It's an ideal option if you want a premium laptop with the latest specs Intel and NVIDIA have to offer.

Acer Swift X (2024)
Editor's choice

The Acer Swift X is a premium creator laptop with excellent specs. It comes with up to an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, 32GB of RAM, and up to 1TB storage. This laptop also has multiple AI features and a beautiful OLED display that's just perfect for creatives.

$1200 at Acer

Now, that doesn't mean the Swift Go is a bad choice. It also has Intel's latest processors, AI capabilities, a decent display, and a good battery life. The Swift Go is an excellent choice if you're on a budget, not looking to play games, and looking for a simple productivity laptop.

Acer Swift Go 14 (2024)
Budget option

The Acer Swift Go has Intel's latest processors, up to 32GB of RAM, an OLED display with a 90Hz refresh rate, and up to 1TB storage. This laptop is an excellent choice for on-the-go productivity and is portable, well-performing, and endowed with AI features.

$1000 at Amazon$1000 at Acer

item.title: How to run Llama 2 locally on your Mac or PC
item.itunes:title: How to run Llama 2 locally on your Mac or PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Requirements
  • How to run Llama 2 on a Mac or Linux using Ollama
  • How to run Llama 2 on Windows using a web GUI

If you like the idea of ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, or any of the other AI assistants, then you may have some concerns relating to the likes of privacy, costs, or more. That's where Llama 2 comes in. Llama 2 is an open-source large language model developed by Meta, and there are variants ranging from 7 billion to 70 billion parameters.

Given that it's an open-source LLM, you can modify it and run it in any way that you want, on any device. If you want to give it a try on a Linux, Mac, or Windows machine, you can easily!

Requirements

You'll need the following to run Llama 2 locally:

  • One of the best Nvidia GPUs (you can use AMD on Linux)
  • An internet connection
Related
Best GPUs in 2024: Our top graphics card picks
Picking the right graphics card can be difficult given the sheer number of options on the market. Here are the best graphics cards to consider.

How to run Llama 2 on a Mac or Linux using Ollama

If you have a Mac, you can use Ollama to run Llama 2. It's by far the easiest way to do it of all the platforms, as it requires minimal work to do so. All you need is a Mac and time to download the LLM, as it's a large file.

Step 1: Download Ollama

The first thing you'll need to do is download Ollama. It runs on Mac and Linux and makes it easy to download and run multiple models, including Llama 2. You can even run it in a Docker container if you'd like with GPU acceleration if you'd like to have it easily configured.

Once Ollama is downloaded, extract it to a folder of your choice and run it.

Step 2: Download the Llama 2 model

Once Ollama is installed, run the following command to pull the 13 billion parameter Llama 2 model.

ollama pull llama2:13b

This may take a while, so give it time to run. It's a 7.4GB file and may be slow on some connections.

Step 3: Run Llama 2 and interact with it

Next, run the following command to launch and interact with the model.

ollama run llama2

This will then launch the model, and you can interact with it. You're done!

How to run Llama 2 on Windows using a web GUI

If you're using a Windows machine, then there's no need to fret as it's just as easy to set up, though with more steps! You'll be able to clone a GitHub repository and run it locally, and that's all you need to do.

Step 1: Download and run the Llama 2 Web GUI

If you're familiar with Stable Diffusion and running it locally through a Web GUI, that's what this basically is. oobabooga's text generation Web UI GitHub repository is inspired by that and works in very much the same way.

  1. Download the repository linked above
  2. Run start_windows.bat, start_linux.sh, or start_macos.sh depending on what platform you're using
  3. Select your GPU and allow it to install everything that it needs

Step 2: Access the Llama 2 Web GUI

From the above, you can see that it will give you a local IP address to connect to the web GUI. Connect to it in your browser and you should see the web GUI.

Click around and familiarize yourself with the UI. You'll have first loaded a chat window, but it won't work until you load a model.

Step 3: Load a Llama 2 model

Now you'll need to load a model. This will take some time as it will need to download it, but you can do that from inside of the Web GUI.

  1. Click the Model tab at the top
  2. On the right, enter TheBloke/Llama-2-13B-chat-GPTQ and click Download
  3. If it's downloading, you should see a progress bar in your command prompt as it downloads the relevant files.
  4. When it finishes, refresh the model list on the left and click the downloaded model.
  5. Click Load, making sure that model loader says GPTQ-for-LLaMa

It may take a moment for it to load, as these models require a lot of vRAM.

Step 4: Interact with Llama 2!

All going well, you should now have Llama 2 running on your PC! You can interact with it through your browser in a no-internet environment, so long as you have the hardware necessary to execute it. On my RTX 4080 with 16GB of vRAM it can generate at nearly 20 tokens per second, which is significantly faster than you'll find on most free plans for any LLMs like ChatGPT or otherwise.

If you wanted, you could also try to use LM Studio, as there are pre-built models available using Llama 2.

Related
Run local LLMs with ease on Mac and Windows thanks to LM Studio
If you want to run LLMs on your PC or laptop, it's never been easier to do thanks to the free and powerful LM Studio. Here's how to use it

item.title: How to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11
item.itunes:title: How to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There are a lot of excellent web browsers you can use in Windows 11, and one of those is Microsoft Edge. Even though Microsoft Edge is powered by the same engine as Google Chrome, and offers plenty of cool features like access to Bing Chat, and even money-saving shopping features, it's also a critical part of Windows itself, powering some components like Widgets, and even Copilot.

But we understand that not everyone might be a fan of using Microsoft Edge. Unfortunately, though, the browser is pre-installed on all new laptops and Windows 11 PCs as part of Windows itself. What's worse, Microsoft makes it very hard to uninstall Edge, at least in the United States. Thankfully, it's still possible to do it with some work.

If you live in the European Economic Area, however, things are a bit easier thanks to the recent Digital Markets Act. Microsoft is being forced to make it possible to uninstall Edge normally, so we'll have guidance for that below.

How to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11

To get started with removing Microsoft Edge in Windows 11, you're going to have to open the registry editor. Once open, you'll have to use the sidebar to navigate and change a few keys. You can then head into the Windows 11 Settings app and remove Edge like any other app.

You will need administrator privileges to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11 using this method.

  1. Open the Windows 11 Start Menu
  2. Search for Registry Editor and pick the top resulting app.
  3. In the sidebar, click through to the following path: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE > SOFTWARE > WOW6432Node>Microsoft>Windows>CurrentVersion>Uninstall>Microsoft Edge
  4. Once there, right-click on NoRemove in the primary pane and choose Modify.
    1. In more recent versions, this value may not exist. Instead, right-click an empty area and choose New > DWORD (32-bit) Value and name it NoRemove.

  1. In the window that opens, change the Value Data to 0 and then click OK. If you just created the value, it should be set to 0 by default, so there's no need to change it.
  2. Close the Registry Editor and restart your PC.
  3. Open the Windows 11 Settings app.
  4. Choose Apps.
  5. Choose Installed Apps.
  6. Find Microsoft Edge in the list choose the three dots and then Uninstall.
  7. Click Uninstall again to confirm your choice, and once more in the last dialog that appears.

Your PC will remove Microsoft Edge and you'll have to restart your PC. Microsoft does warn you that certain system features may not work as expected if you remove Edge, so if you notice anything weird, you may need to install it again

Do note that Microsoft Edge will try to reinstall itself at some point, so if you want to prevent that from happening, follow the steps in the final section below.

Uninstalling Edge if you're in the EEA

If you live in a country that's part of the European Economic Area or you've set up your laptop's region to one of these countries, Microsoft has been forced by the Digital Markets Act to allow you to uninstall Edge without any fiddling. As such, you don't have to mess with the Registry Editor to uninstall Edge, so you can start from step 8 above to just uninstall Edge as you would any other app. Do note that you'll need to be running the latest version of Windows 11 for this to work.

Uninstall Edge using Windows Terminal

If you'd rather use a command line to uninstall Edge, you have that option, too. The easiest method is to use Windows Terminal, which encompasses both Windows PowerShell and the classic Command Prompt. We'll be using the default PowerShell profile for our example.

  1. Right-click the Start menu icon (or Press Windows + X on your keyboard) and choose Terminal (Admin).

    This option may be labeled as Windows Terminal (Admin) or Windows PowerShell (Admin) depending on your version of Windows.

  2. Enter the following command: 
    Get-AppxPackage *edge*
  3. This will give you information about the Edge package. Look for the PackageFullName line and copy the text next to it.
  4. Enter the following command, followed by the text you just copied: 
    Remove-AppxPackage
    So, for instance, if the PackageFullName field is Microsoft.MicrosoftEdgeDevToolsClient_1000.22621.1.0_neutral_neutral_8wekyb3d8bbwe, you would enter: Remove-appxpackage Microsoft.MicrosoftEdgeDevToolsClient_1000.22621.1.0_neutral_neutral_8wekyb3d8bbwe
  5. Press Enter and wait for the process to finish.
  6. Restart your computer.

Again, Edge may try to reinstall itself, so follow the steps below.

Prevent Microsoft Edge from reinstalling

  1. Open the Windows 11 Registry Editor.
  2. In the sidebar, click through to navigate the following paths: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINES>SOFTWARE>Microsoft.
  3. Right-click on the Microsoft folder.
  4. Choose New and then Key.
  5. Right-click on the Key and rename it to EdgeUpdate.
  6. Right-click on EdgeUpdate and choose New and then DWORD (32-bit) Value.
  7. Right-click on the new Value you created (It will be named New Value #1)
  8. Select Rename and name it DoNotUpdateToEdgeWithChromium.
  9. Then, right-click on it choose Modify, and then change Value Data to 1. Press OK.
  10. Restart your PC.

You don't have to uninstall Edge

As you can tell, Microsoft doesn't make it easy to remove the Edge web browser in Windows 11. You shouldn't really have to uninstall it anyway, especially if you're already using another browser like Firefox, Chrome, or Opera. The only real reason to remove it would be if you're running extremely low on disk space, or if you really don't want to have Edge on your PC for whatever reason.

item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of excellent web browsers you can use in Windows 11, and one of those is Microsoft Edge. Even though Microsoft Edge is powered by the same engine as Google Chrome, and offers plenty of cool features like access to Bing Chat, and even money-saving shopping features, it's also a critical part of Windows itself, powering some components like Widgets, and even Copilot.

But we understand that not everyone might be a fan of using Microsoft Edge. Unfortunately, though, the browser is pre-installed on all new laptops and Windows 11 PCs as part of Windows itself. What's worse, Microsoft makes it very hard to uninstall Edge, at least in the United States. Thankfully, it's still possible to do it with some work.

If you live in the European Economic Area, however, things are a bit easier thanks to the recent Digital Markets Act. Microsoft is being forced to make it possible to uninstall Edge normally, so we'll have guidance for that below.

How to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11

To get started with removing Microsoft Edge in Windows 11, you're going to have to open the registry editor. Once open, you'll have to use the sidebar to navigate and change a few keys. You can then head into the Windows 11 Settings app and remove Edge like any other app.

You will need administrator privileges to uninstall Microsoft Edge in Windows 11 using this method.

  1. Open the Windows 11 Start Menu
  2. Search for Registry Editor and pick the top resulting app.
  3. In the sidebar, click through to the following path: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE > SOFTWARE > WOW6432Node>Microsoft>Windows>CurrentVersion>Uninstall>Microsoft Edge
  4. Once there, right-click on NoRemove in the primary pane and choose Modify.
    1. In more recent versions, this value may not exist. Instead, right-click an empty area and choose New > DWORD (32-bit) Value and name it NoRemove.

  1. In the window that opens, change the Value Data to 0 and then click OK. If you just created the value, it should be set to 0 by default, so there's no need to change it.
  2. Close the Registry Editor and restart your PC.
  3. Open the Windows 11 Settings app.
  4. Choose Apps.
  5. Choose Installed Apps.
  6. Find Microsoft Edge in the list choose the three dots and then Uninstall.
  7. Click Uninstall again to confirm your choice, and once more in the last dialog that appears.

Your PC will remove Microsoft Edge and you'll have to restart your PC. Microsoft does warn you that certain system features may not work as expected if you remove Edge, so if you notice anything weird, you may need to install it again

Do note that Microsoft Edge will try to reinstall itself at some point, so if you want to prevent that from happening, follow the steps in the final section below.

Uninstalling Edge if you're in the EEA

If you live in a country that's part of the European Economic Area or you've set up your laptop's region to one of these countries, Microsoft has been forced by the Digital Markets Act to allow you to uninstall Edge without any fiddling. As such, you don't have to mess with the Registry Editor to uninstall Edge, so you can start from step 8 above to just uninstall Edge as you would any other app. Do note that you'll need to be running the latest version of Windows 11 for this to work.

Uninstall Edge using Windows Terminal

If you'd rather use a command line to uninstall Edge, you have that option, too. The easiest method is to use Windows Terminal, which encompasses both Windows PowerShell and the classic Command Prompt. We'll be using the default PowerShell profile for our example.

  1. Right-click the Start menu icon (or Press Windows + X on your keyboard) and choose Terminal (Admin).

    This option may be labeled as Windows Terminal (Admin) or Windows PowerShell (Admin) depending on your version of Windows.

  2. Enter the following command: 
    Get-AppxPackage *edge*
  3. This will give you information about the Edge package. Look for the PackageFullName line and copy the text next to it.
  4. Enter the following command, followed by the text you just copied: 
    Remove-AppxPackage
    So, for instance, if the PackageFullName field is Microsoft.MicrosoftEdgeDevToolsClient_1000.22621.1.0_neutral_neutral_8wekyb3d8bbwe, you would enter: Remove-appxpackage Microsoft.MicrosoftEdgeDevToolsClient_1000.22621.1.0_neutral_neutral_8wekyb3d8bbwe
  5. Press Enter and wait for the process to finish.
  6. Restart your computer.

Again, Edge may try to reinstall itself, so follow the steps below.

Prevent Microsoft Edge from reinstalling

  1. Open the Windows 11 Registry Editor.
  2. In the sidebar, click through to navigate the following paths: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINES>SOFTWARE>Microsoft.
  3. Right-click on the Microsoft folder.
  4. Choose New and then Key.
  5. Right-click on the Key and rename it to EdgeUpdate.
  6. Right-click on EdgeUpdate and choose New and then DWORD (32-bit) Value.
  7. Right-click on the new Value you created (It will be named New Value #1)
  8. Select Rename and name it DoNotUpdateToEdgeWithChromium.
  9. Then, right-click on it choose Modify, and then change Value Data to 1. Press OK.
  10. Restart your PC.

You don't have to uninstall Edge

As you can tell, Microsoft doesn't make it easy to remove the Edge web browser in Windows 11. You shouldn't really have to uninstall it anyway, especially if you're already using another browser like Firefox, Chrome, or Opera. The only real reason to remove it would be if you're running extremely low on disk space, or if you really don't want to have Edge on your PC for whatever reason.

item.title: Anker 10-in-1 USB-C hub is now 36% off, falling to its lowest price ever
item.itunes:title: Anker 10-in-1 USB-C hub is now 36% off, falling to its lowest price ever
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Anker USB C Hub
$30 $47 Save $17

This compact USB-C hub is perfect for travel and provides a wealth of connectivity options. The USB-C hub is now on sale for a limited time, with a discount that knocks 36% off. So get it while you can, because at this price, this deal won't last long.

$30 at Amazon

Laptops have gotten really good over the past few years, offering a wealth of performance and features in a compact and slim package. Of course, there are some compromises if you're going with a laptop, which could mean the lack of ports, but USB-C hubs are a great way to expand connectivity, while still keeping your overall footprint quite small. If you've been looking for a great USB-C hub, this Anker 10-in-1 is going to be a fantastic option, and is now on sale with a discount that knocks 36% off for a limited time.

Related
Best USB-C hubs in 2024
Need a USB-C hub for laptop? We've selected the best USB-C hubs on the market. Our top recommendations include hubs from Anker, HP, & more.

What's great about this Anker USB-C hub?

The first thing that's going to pop out at you with this compact USB-C hub is the sheer number of ports, coming in at ten, which is impressive if you're looking to easily expand the connectivity of your laptop. The laptop has a USB-C port with support for PowerDelivery that can be used to charge compatible laptops, along with a second USB-C port that can be used for data transfers. In addition, you get three 5Gbps USB-A ports, SD and microSD card slots, Ethernet, HDMI, and a VGA port.

The hub will support monitor output up to 4K at 30Hz, and can also output to two monitors if needed. While you get a lot of ports, this hub is still quite compact, which means it's perfect if you need to take it with you on a trip or just want to take it to the office. Although this hub is meant for a laptop, you can also use it for other devices as well, like smartphones, tablets and PC gaming handhelds. And since this is an Anker product, you're going to get great reliability and an 18-month warranty to provide some added peace of mind.

This is going to be an excellent addition to any product that needs extra ports. While its normal price comes in at $64.99, it can now be had for an absolute steal, with a discounted price that comes in at $29.99 for a limited time. Of course, if you're looking for something more robust, then it's a good idea to look into some of our docking station recommendations.

item.title: How AI could unlock our ability to look back into the past
item.itunes:title: How AI could unlock our ability to look back into the past
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • AI is revolutionizing our ability to translate and understand lost languages, providing insights into ancient civilizations and philosophies.
  • Through advanced pattern analysis, AI can unravel complex linguistic structures that humans may struggle to decipher on their own.
  • AI-driven historical reconstructions offer a unique opportunity to preserve cultural heritage and educate future generations about the past.

Following the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 A.D., several Roman towns were destroyed, including the famous city of Pompeii. The Vesuvius Challenge was founded in 2023, a competition to "decipher Herculaneum scrolls using 3D X-ray software." This refers to the scrolls that were carbonized by intense heat and buried under rock for centuries. Now, thanks to artificial intelligence and machine learning, we're finally figuring out what these scrolls said.

Computer vision and machine learning can help identify patterns

Humans would never be capable

Source: Vesuvius Challenge

The best and most advanced example we have currently is the team of winners of the Vesuvius Grand Prize. Three students built a model that could decypher the contents of a scroll, validating previous findings and making significant new ones. The challenge was to recover four passages of 140 characters of text, and the team managed that along with 11 other passages, totaling over 2000 characters of text.

The scroll itself contains never-before-seen text and is not a duplicate of any other existing work. It discusses Epicurean philosophy and the value of pleasure in goods that are available. It's a fascinating insight into a period in a place that we know starkly little about in comparison to recent centuries. Now with machine learning entering the fold and giving us our first look at these scrolls, we're already learning a lot.

The goal for this year's Vesuvius Challenge is to go from being able to read 5% of this one scroll to 90% of all four scrolls. That's a tall ask, but then again, the team behind the Vesuvius Challenge never thought that a team would be able to get as much as this one did. AI was already being used to restore text from damaged Greek inscriptions, but this is the first time it's being used to read entirely unopened scrolls.

AI can help us translate lost languages

Pattern analysis is something AI is really good at

Source: Google

The method AI uses to decipher unknown languages is based on detecting patterns and analyzing the structure of languages. It's adept at spotting consistent linguistic rules and patterns, even when the meanings of words are not immediately obvious. This ability enables AI to hypothesize about the grammar, syntax, and lexicon of languages that have never been decoded, drawing on its analysis of known languages for clues. The more data AI has to work with, the better it becomes at interpreting and translating these languages, transforming obscure symbols and scripts into understandable content.

That's why Google's AI Lab, in collaboration with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), developed a machine learning program aimed at translating lost languages. They first translated Linear B, a language that had been translated already but was translated for the first time using a machine. Google's Gemini 1.5 can also be taught to translate rare languages with just a grammar book to the same level that a human would if they learned from that same book.

Related
5 things Gemini 1.5 Pro can do that 1.0 couldn't
Gemini 1.5 is here, and here is 5 things that Gemini 1.5 Pro can do that 1.0 couldn't

AI's capacity to handle large volumes of text quickly and efficiently far surpasses what human scholars can achieve on their own. It can sift through extensive datasets in moments, identifying linguistic features and patterns that would take humans much longer to notice. This speed and scale don't just speed up the process of uncovering new languages; they also open up possibilities for discovering connections between languages that weren't previously recognized. Not to mention machines don't get fatigued — at least, not in the way humans do.

Nonetheless, employing AI to translate unknown languages comes with its set of obstacles. The quality and scope of the data AI learns from can significantly influence the precision of its translations, and the nuanced understanding of human experts is essential to oversee and confirm what AI suggests. Despite these challenges, the partnership between AI capabilities and human insight offers a promising avenue for illuminating languages that have remained unspoken for ages.

Simulating and modeling historical sites and streets

Helping humans rebuild important places

Source: Google

The use of AI-driven simulations and 3D modeling is revolutionizing the field of historical research and preservation, enabling the digital reconstruction of damaged or lost historic sites and artifacts. These technologies allow for the detailed analysis of archaeological data and historical records to create accurate digital models. Such reconstructions reveal the architectural styles, construction methods, and materials used by past civilizations, providing a glimpse into how these sites once appeared and were utilized. These recreations are essential for piecing together historical contexts and understanding the significance of these sites during their peak. This is currently underway in Europe thanks to the European Union, and Google also did the same a few years ago in reconstructing historical streets.

Additionally, AI reconstructions extend beyond academic purposes, playing a crucial role in cultural preservation and education. They enable the creation of detailed, interactive models of heritage sites that are no longer accessible, preserving their legacy for future generations. Through virtual tours and interactive experiences, people worldwide can explore and learn about these historical sites, promoting a global appreciation of our shared cultural heritage. This approach is especially vital for sites threatened by environmental factors, conflict, or decay, helping to ensure they are not permanently lost.

The implications of AI in historical restoration are profound, providing exact models for conservators and restoration experts to guide their work, ensuring authenticity and accurate representation of cultural heritage. As AI technology evolves, its contribution to historical preservation is set to increase, offering more advanced tools for exploring, preserving, and experiencing our history. These digital reconstructions offer a dynamic way to engage with history, effectively narrowing the gap between the past and the present.

What the future holds about the past

There's a lot more to come

With the advent of everything AI in the last year, we expect that there's only more to come in this field. Companies are really only getting started with generative AI in the grand scheme of things, and with an ancient scroll partially decoded from an object where it wasn't thought possible, it's an exciting time to be a history buff. AI has a tendency to hallucinate, but that's when left to its own devices; researchers, historians, and scientists know what they're looking for, and act as a strong guiding arm in the process.

I'm personally excited to learn more about the past as we develop these technologies further, and they may give people an interest in history that they previously didn't know they had.

Related
Best AI PCs in 2024
With the addition of an integrated NPU, these PCs will take your AI applications to the next level of efficiency

item.title: Cooler Master sues SilverStone and Enermax over patent infringement on AIO liquid coolers
item.itunes:title: Cooler Master sues SilverStone and Enermax over patent infringement on AIO liquid coolers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Cooler Master is heating things up by taking SilverStone and Enermax to court over alleged patent breaches.
  • AIO technology, with liquid already in the system, is at the center of the patent dispute in the cooling hardware scene.
  • The lawsuit alleges Cooler Master's ownership of key cooling technologies, potentially impacting future cooler designs.

Despite being in the business of keeping things cool, things are heating up in the PC cooling hardware scene. Cooler Master is taking both SilverStone and Enermax over beliefs that they have breached one of Cooler Master's patents, while also suing Apaltek under the belief that the company supplied the prior two with the technology.

Related
Cooler Master MasterBox NR600 review: High airflow PC case on a budget
Cooler Master's budget-friendly mid-tower PC chassis provides decent thermal performance

Cooler Master takes SilverStone and Enermax to court

As reported on TechPowerUp, Cooler Master believes that SilverStone and Enermax sold products that used its patented "All In One" (AIO) cooling technology. If you're not sure what an AIO cooler is, it's when a manufacturer sells a liquid cooler that requires minimal setup from the user. This includes having the liquid already in the system and ready to go; all you need to do is plug it all in.

It's a key feature for some of the best liquid coolers for CPUs out there, but Cooler Master believes that it owns the patent to the technologies that other companies use:

The Cooler Master suit alleges that the pump-inside-radiator design choice is a Cooler Master invention; as is the ARGB mini-controller. It also alleges infringement in the essential design of the coolers.

The named companies aren't the only ones using AIO technology, but TechPowerUp surmises that Cooler Master may be seeking out smaller companies to start with. The website also got a quote from the defendant, who claimed that Cooler Master was being a "bully." Either way, the outcome of this trial will affect how coolers are designed in the near future.

item.title: Is ChatGPT safe?
item.itunes:title: Is ChatGPT safe?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
In the tech community, the emergence of AI chatbots, especially OpenAI's ChatGPT, has garnered a lot of attention and raised some eyebrows. Thanks to its sophisticated ability to understand and generate text that sounds incredibly human, ChatGPT has become a valuable asset for a wide range of uses. But with its impressive capabilities comes the need for careful scrutiny regarding its safety. How secure is ChatGPT for everyday users, those who develop with it, and the wider online world?

Related
ChatGPT vs Microsoft Copilot vs Google Gemini: What are the differences?
If you've been trying to figure out which generative AI tool is better, you've come to the right place

Data security and privacy

Don't put anything sensitive into ChatGPT

Source: Google DeepMind

A major worry about ChatGPT's safety centers on the protection of personal information and data security. ChatGPT is trained on a broad array of data sources, like books, websites, and various texts, to come up with its replies. OpenAI states that it doesn't keep personal chats or employ them in further training exercises, aside from AI trainers reviewing them in future. The real issue, however, is the potential risk of these dialogues being captured, saved, or exploited by someone else.

To protect users' data, OpenAI has put in place numerous security protocols, including encryption and tight control over access. Nevertheless, it's wise for users to be careful about what they share with any AI-driven chatbot. Refraining from entering into discussions about sensitive, financial, or private matters is recommended, even as the platform continues to improve its security features.

Misinformation and bias

An uphill battle

Safety concerns also extend to the possibility of ChatGPT disseminating false information or showing prejudice. Even with advancements in AI technology, ChatGPT isn't perfect and can produce responses that are either incorrect or biased, reflecting the data on which it was trained. OpenAI is actively striving to minimize these problems. However, it's important for users to scrutinize the responses from ChatGPT and verify them against reliable sources.

To combat misinformation and bias, other companies like Google have been rather open about their struggles that they've had in battling AI going off the rails. OpenAI hasn't been as open about it, but Google has published a list of AI principles that it follows in developing its AI.

Ethical and societal impacts

Training data has to be found somewhere

Source: Unsplash

The ethical and social consequences of ChatGPT and related technologies demand attention. There's a real risk they could be used unethically, whether by generating misleading content, mimicking people, or swaying public opinion. OpenAI has put in place policies and employs AI moderation tools to curb these dangers. Yet, the wider discussions about how AI fits into society and how it should be regulated are still very much alive.

Not only that, but the act of using training data not owned by a company that employs an AI model is currently a grey area in the world of computing. With The New York Times suing OpenAI, it may be the case that AI will shift and change over time from how we know it already. Training data has to be found somewhere.

Developer and platform security

This one's on the apps that use ChatGPT

Developers who incorporate ChatGPT into their apps must prioritize platform security. Key measures include safeguarding API keys, watching out for unusual usage trends, and fortifying apps against any weaknesses that might be leveraged through the chatbot interface. OpenAI offers a set of recommended security practices and guidelines for developers, underlining the critical need for a secure development framework.

ChatGPT is pretty much as safe as it gets

There's not much more you can do

ChatGPT marks a significant leap forward in AI technology, presenting enormous opportunities for creativity and ease of use. Yet, its safety involves complex layers, including concerns over data protection, privacy, the spread of false information, biases, and ethical issues. OpenAI, along with the wider technology sector, is actively working to tackle these challenges. Nonetheless, both users and developers need to approach their interactions with or implementation of ChatGPT with care and responsibility.

Understanding and managing the intricacies of AI in our digital existence is crucial. Being well-informed, alert, and principled is essential to fully leverage the advantages of AI while minimizing its potential dangers.

Related
How to jailbreak ChatGPT: Get it to do anything you want
ChatGPT can do a lot, but it can't do everything

item.title: The Google One AI Premium plan now gives you Gemini access in Gmail and Docs
item.itunes:title: The Google One AI Premium plan now gives you Gemini access in Gmail and Docs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Google One AI Premium subscribers now get a Gemini chatbot for Gmail, Docs, and more.
  • It's a new move that mirrors other paid AI plans, like Copilot Pro.
  • The Google One AI Premium plan costs $19.99/month for advanced Gemini access.

With tech companies around the world racing to introduce their own artificial intelligence, we're also seeing the rise of paid plans. These grant the company's AI assistant some useful additional features, and if they manage to take off, they could become a reliable and secure source of revenue for tech companies.

Google was one of the latest companies to offer a premium plan for their AI model, and now, the company has introduced more features. If you're a Google One AI Premium subscriber, you can now use Google's Gemini chatbot in Gmail, Docs, and more.

Related
Google Gemini Advanced: What it is, how to use it, should you get it
Google's Gemini Advanced is an all-new subscription tier AI platform, and it offers a lot that ChatGPT Plus doesn't.

The Google One AI Premium gets more perks

As announced on Google's blog The Keyword, its AI's premium plan adds Gemini to Gmail, Docs, Slides, Sheets, and Meet. It mirrors the trend of companies offering paid plans for AI-based implementation in their apps. For example, the Copilot Pro plan adds Microsoft's chatbot to its productivity suite, while Adobe Acrobat's AI assistant can sum up large documents.

Google gives a few examples as to how you can use Gemini with its apps:

For instance, you can get help writing a potluck invitation in Gmail, drafting a trip itinerary in Docs, building a family budget in Sheets or creating a vision board for a kitchen remodel in Slides.

If you want to give this a try, you need to be subscribed to the Google One AI Premium plan specifically; this feature currently isn't available on the Basic or Premium plans. A Google One AI Premium Plan costs $19.99 a month.

item.title: How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7
item.itunes:title: How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7 without third-party apps
  • How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7 with Stardock
  • Download the official Windows 7 wallpaper
  • Should you use the Windows 7 look in 2024?

Some of the most significant changes made to Windows 11 by Microsoft are visual ones, like changing the way the Start menu and taskbar appear. For some users, that's great news, as it makes the operating system feel modern and fresh. But for others, the new look of Windows 11 is an unnecessary change that inhibits productivity. Luckily, if you're missing the iconic and familiar look of Windows 7, you can make a few alterations to make your new PC or great laptop look a lot like Windows 7. You can do a lot with third-party tools, but there are also some things you can do with Microsoft's official settings. For all the ways you can make Windows 11 look like Windows 7, follow the steps below.

Related
How do I use Windows 11? A guide to the OS
Is the transition to Microsoft's new operating system a little too jarring? Read our guide to learn how to use Windows 11.

How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7 without third-party apps

Once you're running Windows 11, there's not much you can do to make your operating system look like Windows 7. However, one of the biggest changes in modern versions of Windows shifted the taskbar to the center of the bottom of your screen. You can revert this change and put your taskbar to the left, like on Windows 7, by following these easy steps.

  1. Open the Settings app.
  2. Select the Personalization tab.
  3. Click the Taskbar tab.
  4. Select the Taskbar behaviors menu.
  5. Use the dropdown menu beside Taskbar alignment to select the Left alignment option.

How to make Windows 11 look like Windows 7 with Stardock

Shifting the taskbar to the left is a good start, but you can get the full look of Windows 7 on Windows 11 with Stardock. The company has two different tools, Start11 and WindowBlinds 11, that change the look of the Start menu and the overall operating system. Although these two tools aren't free, they cost $7 and $20 respectively; there are free trials available for both. Here's how to install them and get started with your new Windows 11 look.

Make your Start Menu look like Windows 7 with Start11

Start11 makes your Start Menu look more like Windows 11.

  1. Visit the Stardock website and download the Start11 and WindowBlinds 11 applications. (Click the links here for Start11 and WindowBlinds 11.)
  2. Launch the installation file for Start11 from your downloads folder.
  3. Agree to the license agreement and click the Next button.
  4. Confirm the installation location and click the Next button.
  5. When the application launches, choose the Left aligned option for the taskbar.
  6. Then, confirm your Start menu theme is set to the Windows 7 option.
  7. After that, you're all set using a Windows 7 start menu.

How to make your operating system look like Windows 7 with WindowBlinds 11

WindowBlinds 11 changes the way windows appear to match the iconic look of Windows 7.

  1. Launch the installation file for WindowBlinds 11 from your downloads folder.
  2. Agree to the WindowBlinds 11 license agreement and click the Next button.
  3. Confirm the installation location and click the Next button.
  4. After WindowBlinds 11 launches, select the Download more styles tab.
  5. Find the Aero 11 style in the list, and press Download. This is the most similar theme to stock Windows 7.
  6. Return to the Styles tab and click Apply style to desktop.
  7. Now, your operating system will look like Windows 7.

Download the official Windows 7 wallpaper

The last thing you may want to do before finishing your Windows 7 theme is download the official Windows 7 wallpaper. This is an optional step, but it definitely helps complete the look. Here's how.

  1. Click or right-click the image to open it in full resolution. Scroll down for the images.
  2. Save the images to your Downloads folder.
  3. Find the image in your Downloads folder.
  4. Right-click the image and click Set as desktop background.

Should you use the Windows 7 look in 2024?

After you've completed all these steps, your Windows 11 PC will look a lot like a Windows 7 machine. You'll have changed the desktop wallpaper, fixed the taskbar, reverted the Start menu, and changed window appearance settings at the operating system level. If you're familiar with Windows 7, it might make sense for you to take these steps to make your great PC look like an older one.

Stardock Start11 v2

Stardock Start11 v2 is a great application to personalize your Windows 11 or Windows 10 experience. You can add a floating taskbar, three styles of Start Menu, and even pin folders to your taskbar, and so much more.

$7 at Stardock
WindowBlinds 11

Stardock's WindowBlinds 11 can change the entire look of your Windows 11 operating system. That includes making your Windows 11 PC look like it's still running Windows 7.

$20 at Stardock

  4. Confirm the installation location and click the Next button.
